21. Typical Sequences and Lagre Deviations

21.1. WLLN

Theorem 21.1 (WLLN). Let be a set of i.i.d. random variables with mean and . Then for any ,

(21.1)

i.e. the sample average converges in probability to .

For finite variance we can use Chebyshev’s, which establishes that the rate of convergence is at least . But later we will find out the rate is actually exponential.

21.2. Typical Sequences

Typical sequences are ones that appear with high probability as we generate i.i.d. samples. Consider a sequence with normalized log-likelihood

(21.4)

SUch sequences forms a collection .

The WLLN establishes that

(21.5)

Since typical sequences satisfy , we obtain that they are roughly equiprobable and each occurs with a probability of roughly This implies there are about typical sequences.

For a distribution over alphabet of size , there are possible sequences. Thus whenever , only an exponentially27 small fraction of possible sequences occur with non-negligible probability.

Remark. This provides a new operational interpretation of entropy as characterizing the exponential size of the typical set.

□

Example 21.1 (Data Compression). Instead of compressing each symbol, yielding bits, we compress use bits to specify each typical sequence (give random index if sequence is not typical). This requires few bits whenever i.e. is not uniform. Notice that we compressed arbitrarily close to bits per symbol, and this can be shown to be optimal and thus another “operational interpretation” of entropy.

21.2.1. Continuous distributions

The typical set is similarly well defined after using differential entropy and replacing sums with integrals. We similarly obtain that the typical set has volume and the AEP theorem applies.

21.3. Divergence -typical sequences

Atypical sequences give rise to anomalous beavhior, characterized by large deviations analysis.

Let’s develop a divergence based characterization of typical set that applies equally well to both discrete and continuous distributions. Same setup with and but consider a reference distribution and form a normalized log-likelihood ratio:

(21.10)

Apply WLLN to the random variable to conclude that

(21.11)

and we use this to define divergence -typical sequences, which form the set . .

The two notions of typical set are not strictly equivalent:

But they are essentially equivalent in one important sense. Two sets with probabilities close to one have intersection with probability close to 128. This equivalent behavior is more relevant to our analysis.

This divergene characterization reveals that sequences that are typical with respect to satisfy , yielding

(21.12)

Hence This implies: the probability that sampling from a distribution will produce any distribution in the typical set for is exponential small, with the rate given by the information divergence of from . This yields an operational interpretation of information divergence.

Summary:

  1. The typical set for every nonuniform distribution includes a vanishing subset of the total collection of possible sequences.

  2. The typical sets for different distributions are essentially disjoint. In particular, we have

Remark.

  • The probability of drawing samples assuming independence (i.e. with the marginals) yields for distribtuion is 2^(-N I(xx ; yy)). This yields an operational definition of mutual information.

21.4. Large Deviations of Sample Averages

Let the true data-generating process be . A sample average is close to when the samples are typical of . It deivates when it is atypical of and thus typical for a different distribution . This is an exponential family containing .

The atypical distribution that yields sample mean is dominantly and it belongs to a 1D canonical exponential family that includes the true data-generating distribution.
